Quick note about the Schwarzschild solution: for finitely sized masses, the solution is given for the region outside the black hole and not the one within; when the mass collapses it's given for the entire region, because there is no 'within'.

The problem with black holes leading to white holes:  The assumption is that the mass consumed by a black hole is then expelled in a white hole.  That would mean that the mass would not be retained by the black hole, and thus the black hole would never grow.  But that's not what we're observing.

Something missing from the video: time slows down when measured from the outside, but the time of an object falling into a black hole doesn't change and is called proper time. So obviously the object, from its point of view, falls, but a distant observer can't see (can't measure) it fall. Pretty simple.

I love the waterfall analogy because supercritical (fast) and subcritical (slow) flow in rivers are separated by a critical singularity in a waterfall. Surface waves (such as the ones a pebble would make) can never go up if they are downstream of the critical point, but propagate up and down in slow flow.

Penrose diagrams are outdated. They were made with the idea that black holes exist until the end of time. Thanks to Mr. Hawking we now know that they all have different death-dates. They get smaller over time, and eventually dissipate. Ontop of this, there is no evidence for parallel worlds outside of mathematical theory.

It's impossible to "see" anything falling beyond the "Event horizon" because the only element that can convey what is happening is light. Beyond the "Event horizon," nothing can escape, not even light, which is the only thing that can inform us about the events. However, this differs from what is experienced at the center of the event.

The problem lies in diagrams. When diagrams change or the angle between lines change it doesn't necessarily translate to a change in reality. We can associate meaning to certain parts of the diagram, but not every aspect of the lines means something in universe. For example if I use different coloured pens it doesn't mean space has different colours
When you take the math too literally and don't realize that it always is, by necessity, a rough approximation and description of reality (only), that's when you end up with people claiming all those things are real and "proven". There is one clear misleading thing this video and many others like this fall into: in the graphics, the earths orbit follows the curvature of space-time. **This is not correct** By this logic, a stationary object would not be affected by gravity at all. A photon or any other lightspeed thing would follow the curvature, being only slightly deflected in the direction of the sun.

So the "CMB" is our white hole, and the "big bang" was a supernova. SMBHs were already black holes at the beginning of their time, remnants of a prior closed universe, and likely contain their own universes, while their former accretion disks are now galaxies in our universe. This explains why the JWST sees distant galaxies already fully-formed. "It is turtles all the way down."

General relativity is an interpretion, not a fact. Treating an interpretation as a fact is called reafication or hypostasis, based on abstraction. We can't catalogue every fact of the universe. Some processes are too far away and long ago for us to observe, and we can't see the contents of any black hole. We don't understand dark matter or dark energy. Many physicists put the abstraction ahead of the facts.

The issue here is that this presumes that the Schwarzschild radius stays that same when an object enters it.  If that radius doesn't increase in size, then yes, you would never see an object enter a black hole from the outside.  Adding mass to it would increase the Schwarzschild radius and the difference between the initial and post absorption radii.  With a difference in radii, you could get a situation like that described at 17:10.

Light from objects that are falling into a black hole does not slow down at the event horizon. It will still leave the object  going away from the event horizon traveling at the speed of light, but only red shifted further and further. Of course this is for light traveling normal to the surface of the black hole. Other angles could have it spiraling in, spiraling out, or orbiting. But if itвАЩs traveling directly away, it does so at the speed of light.

7:07 Schwarzschild Coordinates are not spherical, as r can not be a radius. The space-like distance to the center of a Schwarzschild system can be calculated; it is infinite. The r in the metric is actually a geometric coordinate (the radius of curvature) that only works in spherically symmetric geometries. Called the "effective radius", one can find it by putting a circle centered on the BH, finding the circle's circumference and then dividing by 2ѕА.

When something enters a black hole, it is pulled apart by extreme gravity and its matter is crushed into an infinitely dense point called a singularity at the center. For an object falling in, this process is often described as "spaghettification," where it's stretched into a long, thin strand. From the perspective of a distant observer, the object would appear to slow down, become redder, and fade away as it crosses the event horizon, never to be seen again.

The properties of an anti-black hole seems like a description of the visible edge of the universe. Rather than a singularity in space, it's a singularity in time--a boundary receding from all observers that can't ever be reached. Anything that became part of the visible universe at the beginning can't ever leave, as crossing that visible horizon would also require traveling faster than light.   It's an interesting symmetry that makes more sense than a literal "white hole".

Newton disavowing the idea that gravity works across arbitrarily large distances was him giving himself plausible deniability. The thing is: once you assume that the Solar System is held together by gravity, and you already know that the planets orbit the Sun (which Copernicus had figured out already), then it follows that the Earth orbits the Sun. In other words, gravity across long distances implies heliocentrism, and that was still considered a heretical view at the time.

No, it doesn't **predict** white holes, wormholes or anything of the kind.  The mathematics allows such things but it doesn't tell us they exist anymore than it tells us closed timelike curves exist despite the GR solutions that allow it.  It matters how you interpret the math.  No one thinks that GR is a fundamental law and it's far from even clear that the interior of black holes really even collapse to a singularity or if that's just the result of taking an approximate theory too far.

Black hole is just a star consisting of heaviest element in the universe where all known particles fuse together past their strong force. Gravity is as if you put a solid object into a confined space filled with water. Depending on how much water that object displaces, the force that counteracts on the object is gravity. The closer the particles are, the heavier the element becomes, the stronger the displacement of dark matter which in turn counteracts stronger, creating gravitational pull.
That's what you get when you mix physics and mathematics. There is a limit where physics and mathematics cancel each other out, and the part that is touched by mathematics is not part of our reality or the reality of this universe. The Penrose-Einstein diagram is a distorted representation of a steady black hole (as told by Veritasium on the video) because the singularity is shown as a line (instead of a point as it is on real life) impossible to avoid once inside of the horizon    The same happens when you use the same Penrose-Einstein diagram to show the singularity of a rotating black hole as a couple of parallel lines (instead of a 3D ring) easily avoidable. So places that seem to be able to reach may actually not.

The classical escape velocity equation is sqrt(2GM/r). The mass is the density times the volume. M = rho*(4/3)*pi*R^3. When r = R we get the escape velocity at the mass's surface is R * sqrt((8/3)*GM*rho*pi). This says we can have a black hole made out of something the density of marshmallows if you make R big enough. 24:45, I don't see the big deal about the "parallel universe": Far from being something special to black holes and relativity, isn't it merely a consequence of not having enough dimensions in this simplified model? We're talking about a 2D representation of space-time here which is a 1D world progressing in time. You can't get around an object in a 1D world. As soon as you have more dimensions you can get around the object and so the "parallel universes" disappear because they're just another connected portion of our universe.

All the concepts in the video are solely based on the GR equations, of which we know are false, since GR is not compatible with quantum mechanics. And the вАЮimagesвАЬ of black holes are not really images and proof for BH. There is definitely a nomenclature problem with a multiverse (multiple universes).  The term Universe denotes all that exists into a combined set.  Therefore Universe should be moved up to describe what we presently call multiverse, and we can name our "universe" something like Discrete Realm Alpha, and so on. Also, Universe is all inclusive in a way that allows for future discovery and changes of theory, whereas multiverse may not fit with reality. No, that doesn't happen. Black holes would never form in the first place if time stopped at the event horizon. When a star goes supernova, a quantum-sized black hole will form at its centre because of the pressure. But all other matter would stop falling in. Then Hawking radiation would vaporize the black hole. Then another quantum-sized black hole form and be vaporized. And so on, until most of the mass of the star is vaporized and all that remains is a neutron star.  And that is one of the problems of time stopping at the event horizon.

If you change a different perspective from the speed of light to the speed of change, the infrared distortion of objects falling into a black hole becomes easier to understand.  You explained this concept at time index 17:00. Photons must jump from graviton to graviton, but if space is moving faster than the photon, then the photon can look like it is stationary or moving backwards, like a bass swimming up stream. This concept is used when describing messenger particles that elementary particles exchange to exchange messages about their change.

No, black holes do not contain true singularities. Singularities are mathematical results of flawed physical theories, and are not objects that exist in the real world.  Here are some reasons why black holes are not singularities: Infinities don't exist: General relativity predicts that a black hole's center is a point of infinite density, but infinities don't exist in the real world.  Counterexamples: There are counterexamples that show that light rays don't end in singularities.  Alternative models: Some alternative models, like gravastars, don't have singularities.

There is no singularity at the center of a black hole. This has been implicit in many papers going back decades. If you actually use the equations of 4d space that relativity gives to calculate, for example, the surface area of successive spheres as one "descends" further and further into a black hole, the further you "descend", the larger the surface area will be. Only when we use a two-dimensional diagram to (erroneously) depict the inside of a black hole using our homogenous space to map points from the 4d space to our 2d diagram do we see a concentration of mass at a central point.

If an object couldnвАЩt move if time stopped then the black hole itself couldnвАЩt move, which is clearly not the case.

Photons are MASS LESS, they do not "FALL IN" to a black hole when your have crossed the event horizon. What happens is that space is twisted so tightly that even when the photons flow outward, they are from their perspective, still going outward from the black hole. But since space is twisted so tightly, that outward flow is being bent back onto the event horizons edge. TLDR, they are on a round about they cant escape from. To the photon its still moving outward. This is also why you cant escape past the event horizon. No matter what direction you go in, space is constantly twisting back on itself.

Now, I get it when people say 'but GR predicts that this should be traversable' sure - but then they offer no remotely viable resolution to all these physical paradoxes.  Maybe we should spend more time finding an interpretation of GR that DOESN'T having things falling into alternate realities, when that blows up mathematics in far more fundamental and intractable ways than trying to find alternate solutions to GR that might leave mathematics reasonably intact?

26:21 The example of negative latitudes makes sense. There are plenty of cases where 0 is just a reference point that can be moved. But here are two examples you that prove that negative solutions arenвАЩt valid. First, take physical quantities. On paper they can be negative, for concepts like money, but in physical reality, if Jonny starts with 5 apples and takes one apple out of the bag every minute, the bag can never have negative apples. Another physical example is temperature. Sure, some units have moved zero вАШupвАЩ for convenience, but on a Kelvin scale, zero is zero and there is no such thing as negative temperature.

One counterintuitive thing about black holes, above a certain size they are actually not very dense.  In fact: pick any constant density that is greater than zero (no matter how small). If you make a large enough sphere with that density(it will probably be ludicrously large), at a certain size it will collapse into a black hole.  This scenario is oversimplified of course. And doesn't account for pressure or any other forces except gravity. But the common way I have heard this idea expressed in more pop-sci terms is: if you filled the entire solar system with cotton candy (candy floss), then it would collapse into a black hole.

I have a theory that there is no 'matter' in a black hole. It's just energy. or just...nothing.  Electromagnetic radiation cannot escape a horizon. Neither can nuclear weak force, strong force, or any other force.   So, an atom, that crosses the horizon, as it is half-in/half-out, will no longer self-interact. It will fall apart. As will the protons and neutrons.  As will whatever quarks are constituent of.   It should be impossible for any form of causality to occur on the 'infinite' slope within the horizon.  All matter should literally just fall apart, cease to be as it crosses the horizon, and at any point further than that.
*"why can't we have two?"* I'll tell you why ! Distances as mapped in the Penrose diagrams are - well - *distances* and when you pass from positive distances to zero distances into negative distances, it's a problem: *negative distances* _do not really exist_ . so the whole "what's on the other side" question is NOT like having "negative norhternhood" when you pass the north pole. or negative latitude south of the equator; it's like being negative 3 years old. it's like having to grow 2,8 meter to be 1,8 meters tall. You never are _negatively tall_ . You don't ever weigh less than zero grams. Negative distances aren't distances.

Perhaps it's simpler to say that as the spaceship approaches the even horizon, the gravity of the black hole becomes harder and harder to overcome. The result is that at the event horizon photons orbit the black hole indefinitely, forming a still image. After the spaceship crosses the event horizon, its light can't make it out anymore so it's lost forever to people outside of the event horizon. And just above the event horizon, the escape velocity is less that the speed of light though the light we see from it is very red shifted (like the light from a source that was moving away from us at a large speed, though it's not moving away, only its space time is very curved).

Tiny inaccuracy at 9:46: for most stars it is not the radiation pressure but rather the gas pressure that is dominant in counter-balancing the force of gravity. The radiation pressure, which is the pressure exerted by the emitted photons, is usually many orders of magnitude smaller than the gas pressure which is caused by the motion of the atoms themselves. However, radiation pressure is proportional to T^4 whereas gas pressure is simply proportional to T (where T is the temperature). This means that in very heavy stars where the temperature is higher, radiation pressure can indeed become more important than gas pressure. But this is usually very rare.  Otherwise, awesome video!

Time stops at the event horizon of a black hole, but only as seen by someone outside the black hole. Hawking radiation reduces the mass and rotational energy of black holes, leading to their eventual evaporation. Since a black hole evaporates through Hawking radiation, the black hole would cease to exist before anything could cross the event horizon. The evaporation process would reduce the black hole's mass and event horizon, effectively "shrinking" the point of no return. Therefore, equations derived from the perspective of something entering a black hole, such as the Schwarzschild metric or the geodesic equations do not accurately describe the reality of a black hole's event horizon.

Fun fact as fellow artilleryman, when calculating ballistic trajectories you start by pretending there is nothing in a flat 2-d universe except the howitzer, the round, and a constant 'down'-word acceleration. From that start point of the "standard" world, you then add corrections for every error, wind speed/direction/density, humidity, your distance from the equator, the rotation of the earth, wether [sic] you're firing with or against that rotation, the weight of the round, air temperature, and most anything else that could effect any part of the round traveling. It makes logical sense to me that Schwartzchild would take a similarly empty starting approach to solve Einstein's equations.
Parallel universes are irrelevant. If nothing can escape the event horizon of a black hole, its light cone is infinite. Anything that comes under the absolute definition of something is included in the absolute definition of everything. This is what infinity is (parallel universes included, but what's the point of having parallel universes if everything already exists in this one?). However you conceptualise it, infinity is everything, which is what will end up in a black hole. A black hole's past light cone is infinite. The future light cone is also infinite. Logically, this makes parallel universes irrelevant. It's like saying "infinity plus one." "One" is included in infinity...  Right?

All these diagrams assume the black hole is eternal.  We think Hawking Radiation is a thing, though, right? Hawking Radiation makes a black hole lose mass, very slowly, and thus shrink. For any given size black hole there's a fixed amount of radiation it will emit per unit of time.  If you get closer to the event horizon, your time is dilated more, if you look back, stuff elsewhere in the universe is happening at a faster rate. Would that not mean that the closer you get to the event horizon, the more hawking radiation the black hole will emit, growing hotter and hotter as you approach the event horizon?  Wouldn't that mean the black hole would 'shrink away' under your feet as you're about to fall into it?

Moving back and forth between general relativity and quantum mechanics is possible by reconsidering pi. If the speed of light is constant, pi changes, and the value of pi is different in weak gravitational fields. In a supermassive gravitational field, the speed of light is constant, so time must stop. And time stops for the photon at the point of interaction. Therefore, pi changes to ѕА = вИЪ8 = 2вИЪ2. Einstein's equation of general relativity then becomes GќЉќљ=[ѕА^3 /c^4]GTќЉќљ. We can imagine gravitational particles as rings, with a string passing through a hole in the ring connecting a point at infinity in the past to a point at infinity in the future. This fluctuation is like pulling and threading a ring through a puzzle.

32:40 This is sort of correct but a bit misleading. What's happening is that the singular ring in the rotating black hole is _gravitationally repulsive._ And this repulsive action is felt already in the "standard" (r>0) universe inside the inner horizon. But in the "negative universe" there are no horizons so the repulsion from the ring can be felt everywhere in it, and not just within certain restricted region. BTW, that "negative space" has an even more bizarre feature: a roughly toroidal region next to the singular ring with the property that travelling around it (running in circles) moves you backwards in time! This is known as "Carter's time machine" and seems to indicate the "negative space" cannot exist. Anyway.

Another issue that isn't covered here is that if you passed through into that antiverse you mentioned, you could continue tiling the universes, black holes and white holes laterally into infinity - and if you did that then you could enter the same universes from both sides of singularities. From one side you'd enter via a white hole from a universe, the other via a different white hole from an antiverse, where gravity would work backwards. What if certain things or ideas only exist because of the specific lens through which we view them? By changing that lensвАФour perspectiveвАФwhat we once saw might vanish entirely. And what if our scientific methods aren't the only valid approaches? The big thing is, black holes were using less levels of abstraction than the later results that inform wormholes white holes and other universes. By that point the abstraction has become too much to represent actual reality.   ItвАЩs a mathematical slippery slope to continue extrapolating by simply warping the coordinate system. At some point you have to reconstrain things to conform with observed principles. Among them, the issues of tidal gravitation effects to matter, the incorrect behavior of treating Bosons as matter, etc.   вАЬYouвАЭ canвАЩt move around the inner horizon, because matter comes all the way apart before then. And Bosons canвАЩt вАЬmove aroundвАЭ because they can only follow geodesics which never get to the infinity edges.
One could also say that Gravity is also the changing of an object's physical and quantum reference to other objects attracted to and/or orbiting around it.  When an object is orbiting another object, it is still going straight because that's the direction the object was initially going before it became affected by Gravity. When two objects of such massive size orbit each other, the forces exerted are a by-product of the object's current isolated frame of reference, thus meaning that an object goes from being translated in Flat Geometry to Spherical Geometry the moment their spatial curvatures intersect. Gravity is a Force yes, but it's also an inertial frame of reference used to govern the mass of objects relative to each other.

Fascinating video!  What strikes me is that some of these hidden structures (like in KruskalвАУSzekeres coordinates) remind me of an overlooked step in the history of relativity.  Originally, Voigt and Lorentz derived a velocity-dependent multiplier n(v) when transforming MaxwellвАЩs equations. Later, Einstein and Poincar√© fixed n(v) = 1 for reasons of symmetry and group closure. Elegant, yes вАФ but perhaps premature.  By forcing n(v) = 1, we may have lost the same kind of variable scaling freedom that KruskalвАУSzekeres later revealed to be essential for understanding black hole geometry. In the observable universe something similar happens when we look outward, as we view objects from the center we see a red shift, it an illusion that is apparrent as we view events toward the event horizon. I often think of light as similar to sound, only that sound requires a material medium for propagation. The redshift and blueshift would be the equivalent of an ambulance with its siren on going away (sound pitches down) and towards (sound pitches up) an observer respectively. This doesn't make the wave travel slower or faster but rather the rate at which it is observed (its frequency) is affected by relative velocity. I think the whole space-time bending solution to gravity is just moving the mystery of gravity from "why do bodies attract each other?" to "why do bodies bend space-time?". The Schwarzschild solution describes what someone would see at a near-infinite distance.-- but it doesn't describe what you would see at a distance of, say twice the Schwarzschild radius. At that distance , you would be able to watch what someone does inside of the so-called event horizon. Light from just inside the radius folds back to the black hole, but  it  would, for a while, make it  past 2R before curving back. While you might say that 2R is a small distance, that is only true for stellar black holes and  smaller.  For a large enough supermassive black hole, that distance can be up to a light-month.  Someone at that distance could watch someone pass the horizon and *send a description of what is happening just inside the horizon to the outside universe at an infinite distance.*

Feels to me that everything essentially freezing in time at the moment it crosses the event horizon is the same reason we know that the surface of the black hole is what maintains all of the information of what fell inside of it.  To the perspective of the black hole traveler, they go inside the black hole with no issues (other than possibly being torn apart by tidal forces), but to anybody outside the black hole (i.e. the rest of the universe), you're frozen in time, smeared onto the event horizon like a time capsule that will last until the final moments before the black hole evaporates in an endless amount of years. The information is still there, on the surface as it radiates away and takes bits of that information with it, ultimately preserving information as entropy reaches infinity.

These are not real (energy) value objects in that the real value is borrowed from the dynamic to create the focus, not be in that focus. When you cross the event horizon you cross an elementary potential that closes and annihilates matter to background energy.  It's a passive object. I wonder if some of these limits will be overcome by developing math further.... Mathematics is essentially a language that we've made up to describe reality, and while mathematics has been systematically created to be consistent to reality, that doesnвАЩt necessarily mean that reality has to be consistent to mathematics. That's what I believe, anyway. Rather like if english is missing a word to describe something, that doesnвАЩt mean that that thing can't exist. Or a machine operating according to the principles of physics cannot violate said physics, and is built to operate according to said physics, but that doesnвАЩt mean that only things the machine can do are possible. Mathematics is a system designed to allow us to predict the behavior of reality, but reality isn't required to conform to mathematics.
It's always worth noting that all of the physics we have is a mathematical model to help us understand how the universe works, and it's not necessarily how things actually work. Whenever you hear about bizarre forms of matter or strange behaviours that are mathematical solutions to physics equations, they could be things we're yet to discover, or they could be demonstrating flaws in our models. Relativity and quantum mechanics are both areas of physics that seem to be reliable, but we know that the two don't agree in certain cases. We already know our theories for relativity and quantum mechanics can't be complete, and likely any unified solution won't be complete either. It's still exciting to speculate about what the maths might actually predict, but until observations are made it's just speculation

The problem with "removing" the singularity at the event horizon is that it only matters to an infinitely time-dilated observer. You're dividing zero by zero. From an outside observer's point of view, it doesn't make any difference what the infalling observer observes, because that observation will be frozen in time. Hence the "frozen star" model of black holes, which seems more reasonable to me than the singularity model (except it's space itself that's "frozen", not the infalling matter).  I don't buy this idea that the perspectives of a free observer and a gravity-bound observer are equally valid. It sounds like the kind of New Age hokum that would naturally have been popular in the Progressive Era of the early 20th century. It's pure speculative interpretation; no basis in evidence or logic at all.

If we, from the outside,  see a person falling into the black hole, the time will freeze just before the person crosses the event horizon, so it would take an infinite time we see the person crossing it. Except black holes are not eternal, they will evaporate in a finite time in the future. So, from our perspective, we would see the black hole disappearing before the person crossing the horizon.  The person falling will be in a strong gravitational field, so he/she will experience time of the outside universe accelerating relative to her own. In other words, the person will witness the far future of the universe, tending to infinity, so with enough time to reach the heat death. In this remote but finite future, all the black holes will evaporate. So, will this person see the black hole in front of her evaporating either?

ThereвАЩs actually some interesting new theories regarding to black hole.   Long story short, the theory claims that singularly and event horizon doesnвАЩt exist.  As time slows with gravity, particles falling towards the Schwarzschild radius actually never reaches it. This is because the the black hole is releasing its energy as Hawking radiation, the Schwarzschild radius of the black hole is continuously reducing. There will always be just the tiniest gap between the particle and the Schwarzschild radius. The black hole is thus just a dense object without an event horizon. ThereвАЩs no singularity that breaks the law of physics, and thereвАЩs no dream.  For those of you who are interested for a better explanation of the theory, hereвАЩs the article  Kawai, Hikaru and Yuki Yokokura. вАЬBlack Hole as a Quantum Field Configuration.вАЭ Universe (2020): n. pag.

It is always said that mass curves space-time, so the curvature of space-time is something that mass does to space-time. But, why not say that space-time curves in the presence of mass, meaning that curvature is a reaction of space-time, something that space-time does in the presence of mass, instead of something mass does to space-time? Then, we could say that objects with mass are not "attracting" one another, but space-time is "pushing" them to one another trying to get flat, as space-time "wants" to be. So, space-time is conglomerating mass and producing empty regions of flattening space-time, which has the appearance of space-time expanding. According to Einstein, two massive bodies, both at rest in space-time, won't move towards each other, because there is no force attracting them to each other. For gravity to manifest, bodies MUST be moving.

The light-cone introduced by Minkovski is actually a 2 dimensional space object with one time dimensional. For notation and unit consistency Minkovski represented the third time dimension of the light-cone by ct. By multiplying time by the speed of light, which is a constant/maximum, all three dimensions of the light-cone have the unit of distance.  One way to look at light-cones is a circle which is a 2D object growing with time in one direction, producing a cone shape. There are theoretical ideas proposed where the light-cone can travel backwards which presents other problems such a the violation of the 2nd law of thermodynamics (ie the Entropy of closed systems). Minkovski simplified and collapsed 3D space into a 2D geometry and then inserted time (ct). Light-cones are much easier to visualise than doing the same thing with 4 dimensions (or even more).

The part I liked the most when studying GR with the constants c and G set to 1 to simplify the equations, is that since the Schwarzschild radius R_S = 2 M, I remember to this day that the mass of the Sun is approximately 1.5km (it's Schwarzschild radius is roughly 3km). Very easy number to remember!  Now, if you want to know its actual mass in a meaningful unit of measurements, say kg, that's easy: figure out from dimensional analysis the combination of G and c which will make a distance into a mass. Well, c is 300 000 000 m/s, easy. G is 6.67 x 10^-11  m^3 kg^-1 s^-2. We want our distance to be mass, G is inversely proportional to mass. distance / G gives us m * kg * s^2 / m^3 so kg s^2 / m^2. Multiply by c^2, that will get rid of the s^2 / m^2. So, the mass of the Sun is 1 500 m times c^2 divided by G, so approximately 2.02 x 10^30 kg. Voil√†! Easy to remember!

The Lorentz equations actually mean to compare four-dimensional space to three-dimensional space. Minkowski spacetime puts lightspeed at zero in all directions. Officially the intent was probably originally to illustrate what reality would look like from the point of view of a single light particle.  Mind you, Einstein scored mostly sixes in school. That's why they are called Lorentz-equations, not Einstein-equations. The most important comment I would make is a general criticism of nearly every approach to this topic.  The most important principle Einstien established was that space and time are essentially a single combined thing.  He not only said this explicitly, his equations assume it.  Thus, any attempt at creating a theory uses some pixelation or makes some tiny unit of area or volume.  People usually pick something like a minimum volume = A, which is calculated using Planck's length.  But GR means that different observers would have different values of A because of the length contraction in GR for different relative speeds.  The only way GR can make that work is by treating space-time as a single entity, because the product of the time and area doesn't change.  Every change in A  is matched by a change in the time.  Any discretization scheme has to do that from the get-go just as a basic requirement.

I think the existence of the second universe is somewhat assuming, since we are only using one spatial dimension in the penrose diagram. That means the space inside the event horizon can completely separate the universe into 2, the same way a line can separate a plane.  But it you change it to use even 2 spatial dimenstions (+ 1 temporal), the triangle of the space inside the event horizon becomes a cone, and then you can see that the "left" and "right" universes are actually just one that wraps around the cone.  Plus if you try to add a creation mechanism to the black hole, you would have a point of creation (the supernova), somewhere inside the space and the event horizon cone will just go up from that point. There will be no downward cone, and hence no white hole, because below it you would just have space going from relatively flat to more and more compressed as the star is formed and starts to collapse.
Hey, wanna know something freaky?  A black hole is the final stage of stellar evolution. According to relevant theories, when a star ages, the fuel (hydrogen) at its core is depleted, and under immense external pressure, the core begins to collapse until it ultimately forms a small, dense celestial body. If this body has a mass greater than three times that of the Sun, it will trigger another collapse. When this contraction causes its radius to reach the Schwarzschild radius, its gravitational pull becomes so strong that not even light can escape, thus becoming an invisible celestial bodyвАФa black hole. The formula for calculating the Schwarzschild radius (in centimeters) is: R = 2GM/c¬≤.  HereвАЩs where things get strange. Scientists had calculated the weight of the universe, and the result showed that our known universe has the length of its own Schwarzschild radius. Conclusion: we live in a massive black hole.

Think of it like this, some of the corners of the die act as the Einstein-Rosen bridge. The other act as the borders of the universe that is impassable, but can be observed. For example, the great voids.  The voids have been hypothesized to be meeting points of our universe and another. What if this was true? You then proceed to another event horizon to be ejected by another white hole into some unknown type of universe. Then you repeat the process again to return to the original universe.

15:42 Is it 14 times larger, or 14 times as large, and are they the same thing? This is one of those things where grammatical and in this case geological terms are misused so often they creep into general use and become the "norm". Logically, if a piece of land is 1 mile in diameter, we know that 14 times 1 mile is 14 miles. !4 times as large equals 14 x 1(mile). 14 times the size of the land = 14 miles. But logically and literally 14 times larger means you have two separate things - the original size 1 mile, then the figure of 14 x 1 = 14.  They are two separate quantities, it's 15 miles. In other words, 14 times larger means 14 times plus the original size. 14 times larger than the original size. rather than 14 times the same measurement. 14 times again as the original size, so 14 x1 (+1). I'm not a mathematician so my math equation might not be right, but you get my drift. It boils down to the words "larger" and "the same".
There is an inaccuracy in the very beginning of the video. Actually, the process of falling on a black hole has been described contradicts any idea of growing of black holes. For a bystander, if nothing gets into a hole, there is no reasons for it to grow.  The key is in essence of Schwarzschild's solution. In fact, a black hole type solution is a single spherically symmetric one. It even doesn't require any masses or energies, so in everyday life all the singularities and event horizons are eliminated by surrounding masses. So, any perturbations of a perfectly symmetric situation affect on the event horizon. And that's what happens, I guess, when any object falls on a black hole: the horizon "deforms" to take an object into and it makes the hole possible to grow. I said guess because I've never done relevant calculations, but that's the best explanation we have produced with group mates while been studying general relativity)

Assuming you are in a spaceship with infinite integrity, what happens when you fall into a black hole is that the black hole evaporates in front of you faster than you can reach its center. The evaporation is due to Hawking radiation, which early models did not consider.  Adding Hawking evaporation also avoids meaningless infinities and gives a more realistic interpretation of that easy-to-ignore singularity at the center of K-K coordinates. An evaporating hole also fully accommodates the infinite time slowing of the object seen by outside observers.  Finally, adding Hawking radiation explains the white hole. It consists of all the matter that entered the black hole before converting into pure energy and exploding in your face as you fall toward it. Due to your ever-increasing time dilation, formerly cool Hawking radiation heats until the entire black hole converts into pure energy in minutes to hours.   You may want to bring sunscreen.

When people try to explain gravity using spacetime curvature (imagine a planet in an orbit around the sun), saying "no action at a distance is required" makes sense and is easily understandable: the planet is simply travelling in a "straight" line in a curved spacetime, there is no force required to explain it.  But let's say Zeus hits Earth's brakes and stops the planet from moving in orbit... if "no action at a distance is required" to explain gravity, my intuition would tell me the planet would just hang there at the same distance from the sun, curvature or not. Why would spacetime curvature itself cause the planet to start moving - 'falling' to the sun?  This part is never explained well with the tarp, bowling ball and tennis ball analogy, because in this example, there is an 'external force' - Earth's gravity, which is causing the tennis ball to fall to the bowling ball. So we're explaining gravity using gravity, making it a bad analogy.
As proven by LIGO, VIRGO, & KAGRA measurements of spacetime impulse propogation, the increase in spacetime curvature propagates from the centroid at "the speed of light." That establishes a relative time constant which limits a runaway collapse of matter into a total singularity. Because spacetime is the stiffest or strongest thing in the universe -- change in spacetime curvature, not mass density, should be the first consideration. The first model ought to be of the formation, from the standpoint of propagation rate for spacetime curvature. From an external viewpoint, as spacetime curvature becomes greater, the process appears to take longer. The logic is analogous to the perpetual operation, whereby a radius is divided in half with each iteration -- that will continue for infinity, and NEVER reach zero. I.e., the volume and mass can never reach total singularity (zero) and disappear, and the slope of spacetime curvature cannot reach infinity.

One subtlety I think is worth highlighting: when you show the Penrose diagramвАЩs 'white hole' and 'parallel universe' regions, the northern-hemisphere analogy works mathematically вАФ yes, the equations allow you to extend the manifold like a southern hemisphere. But hereвАЩs the Chomsky-style twist: just because itвАЩs grammatically coherent in the math doesnвАЩt mean it exists physically. These regions are like вАШcolorless green ideasвАЩ of spacetime вАФ perfectly allowed by the rules of general relativity, but ontologically empty.  So the white hole and parallel universe arenвАЩt just hidden from our view; they likely cannot exist in any universe at all. ItвАЩs a beautiful reminder that math can dream furiously, but reality plays by stricter rules. CCC enthusiasts might even see a poetic echo here: the conformal continuation of one aeon to the next shares the same formal elegance, but unlike the idealised Penrose extension, it could have physical meaning

Similar to how changing the coordinate system for a globe always comes at a cost, I feel like changing coordinate systems to dissolve the black hole's event horizon is what creates those new barriers that feed speculation over white hole and parallel universes.  They are definitely artifacts of the coordinate system we chose, so I can't justify that they have meaning beyond that coordinate system.  For example, a globe can have degenerate coordinates using latitude and longitude systems. When you reach the NORTH pole, there ceases to be an EAST or WEST. If you rotated your entire coordinate system in a way that you can see smooth and even continuity lines inside the arctic circle, then you just shifted your degenerate areas elsewhere on that globe.  Unwrapping a globe into 2D space always creates line segment barriers despite the surface of Earth having no spatial barriers. Crossing where we project those barriers to be doesn't mean walking to a parallel Earth.

Good morning, your information in your explanation and infographic at the beginning of the episode is incorrect.   Source: Amy C. Fredericks and Mike Loewenstein for Ask an Astrophysicist Question ID: 011024a  "Time does not slow down and then go backwards at a black hole's event horizon, but a lot of strange stuff does occur.  If you (in a space ship, for example) were to approach the event horizon and cross it, to a person watching you from a great distance it would look like you moved slower and slower as you got closer and closer to the horizon. To them it would look like you never quite reached the horizon. But this is an illusion caused by the fact that the light you emit from your space ship is taking longer and longer to reach the outside observer. This is due to the black hole's immense gravity. From your own point of view, you reach the horizon and cross it, with nothing special happening at the boundary. But of course, the gravitational forces of the black hole will crush you do death sooner or later"

Leaping from Isaac's discomfort about action at a distance to "Einstein figured out" how to solve that does a disservice to the many mathematician-physicists who invented field theory in between.  Newton's gravity was well understood, long before Einstein, as being mediated by a "gravitational field" that propagated the influence of each body outwards to affect other bodies, avoiding the "action at a distance" problem and correctly describing things like Lagrange points.  Of course, that was still an "instantaneous" field, but those responsible for it could have accommodated the idea of a propagating field (and might even have discovered the problems about which Michelson and Morley removed all doubt earlier, had they done so).  Einstein took seriously the implications of Maxwell's invariant speed, which was crucial, and taught us how to consider a propagating gravity.  But long before he did that, folk knew the speed of light was finite and duly postulated that a compact enough massive body could prevent light escaping - black holes predate Einstein.

White holes are purely theoretical. The existence of white holes is a mathematical consequence of solving the equations that describe black holes, such as the Schwarzschild metric or the geodesic equations. These equations falsely assume that an object can enter a black hole. This means that the solutions derived from the Schwarzschild metric and geodesic equations, including white holes, are mathematically consistent but physically invalid, meaning there is no reason to think white holes may exist.  However, the study of complex numbers was considered a purely theoretical construct, with no apparent practical use. However, as mathematics and physics evolved, complex numbers became a fundamental tool for solving problems in electrical engineering, signal processing, quantum mechanics, and many other fields. The study of complex numbers, like the study of white holes, may seem abstract and disconnected from reality at first, but it can lead to breakthroughs and innovations that transform our understanding of the world and our ability to solve real-world problems.

Regarding the concept of using black holes/white holes to travel between universes, it opens up some bizarre questions.  Matter/energy cannot be created or destroyed, therefore the amount of matter/energy in a given universe is fixed.  But if you travel between universes, you're altering this.  You're decreasing matter/energy in one universe and adding it to the one you travel to.  This has some serious implications if you extrapolate it to extremes.  For example, you are literally altering the Hubble constant of a universe this way.  Negligible mass/energy transfer would cause negligible change, but what if it *wasn't* negligible mass/energy?  You could change the fate of an entire universe by moving significant mass/energy from one universe (perhaps altering it to expand forever when it might otherwise not) to another universe (altering it to end in a "big crunch" when it otherwise might not).  It also brings up another strange possibility.  If there are effectively infinite "other" universes, what's to stop you from harvesting them for infinite mass/energy to sustain your own?

A white hole is *NOT* like the big bang. A white hole exists at only one single location in space. The big bang happened at every location in space.   The white hole is what's known as a "vacuum solution" to the field equations. It makes some simplifying assumptions: That the universe is completely empty without any matter in it, including the interior of the black hole being empty too. It assumes that the black or white hole has always existed eternally forever. Spacetime was just curved in this shape for no reason. Also, it's not spinning or electrically charged and stuff like that.   Physically real black holes form from collapsed stars. They have a finite beginning, and did not exist before the supernova. There's a dead star inside the black hole. Real black holes are going to be spinning, and probably have some electrica charge, etc. etc.  There's no known way for a white hole to form. This mathematical solution is literally just like a movie of a black hole being played backwards. The time coordinate is flipped from positive to negative. It's not impressive when you see the math.

Imagine gravity pulling so intensely that the fabric itself becomes severely twisted вАФ knotting time into a tight loop. Everything beneath the knot is still part of the fabric вАФ it hasnвАЩt vanished like we classically imagine with a singularity. All the matter is still there вАФ not destroyed, just caught in a slow-moving pocket of time, evolving on a timeline so distorted we canвАЩt access it from our frame of observation.  That knot is what we mistake as the event horizon. But maybe itвАЩs not a boundary. Maybe itвАЩs a tangle in time вАФ a region where spacetime folds in on itself so deeply that its contents are simply misaligned with our flow of time.  IвАЩve been working on a theory called the Temporal Dislocation Hypothesis вАФ it proposes that black holes are temporally dislocated regions, not singularities. The story told about Newton is too dramatic. It's much more understandable. Look at the Newton's law of gravity: F = G * m¬єm¬≤ / r¬≤. Newton wasn't worried by what was in  this law, he was worried by what was absent there. Time, any mentioning of time. A simple Gedanken experiment: we know that Earth revolves around Sun. Suppose, some clever thief steals the Sun. Question: how fast will the Earth feel the absence of the Sun? Answer: instantaneously- according to Newton's law. The second mass is absent - the gravity force is zero. You can even go further: suppose, at the other end of the universe occurs something that changes the distribution of masses. How fast the Earth will feel it? Again, instantaneously. And instantaneously means the speed of interaction is infinitely high. And physicists don't like infinity. If somehow the infinity arises in the calculations that usually means that somewhere there is an error in them. That was what worried Newton. Not the interaction itself but the infinite speed of it. Einstein's theory is free of this discrepancy because there is a time component in his famous equation.

There is a slight inaccuracy in your description how an infalling object seems to freeze in its motion and gets redshifted out of vision. You make it look as if it first froze and then slowly disappeared. But the apparent freezing and the redshift have the same origin, gravitational time dilation plus Doppler shift. So by the time the motion got slower by a factor 1/2, light with a wavelength of 400 nm (blue end of visible spectrum) gets redshifted to 800 nm (red end).  If you assume that light emitted by the space craft was only in the optical spectrum (i.e. the shortest wavelength was 400 nm) then by the time motion has slowed down by more than a factor of 1/2, all the light emitted by the space craft will have redshifted to the infrared, i.e. it will already be invisible (unless you use night vision goggles). In practice, an infalling object will never be seen frozen, but only slowed down, before it becomes invisible. And not even terribly slowed down, if only optical frequencies are detected (and no higher frequencies are emitted). Dimming, i.e. intensity loss of the light, will only amplify this effect.
White Holes are "connected" to Black Holes, at least in covariant Loop Quantum Gravity. The idea of a Planck star was proposed by that camp in around 2014. From there, it was found that it is not just a star that could "bounce" a couple of times while collapsing, but spacetime itself! Spacetime can then have white hole solutions that "quantum-mechanically tunnel" to black hole solutions.  When we say that the singularities "don't exist", it is in the sense that we have reached the limit of our current theoretical description - in this case, General Relativity, which naturally doesn't apply at singularity. Something has to replace it, and that something is a quantised version of General Relativity (because we know, that semiclassically, GR is fantastically accurate theory).  So those crossing points in Penrose Diagrams actually "fizzle out" into a foam of quantum uncertainty, where the very concept of spacetime does not apply. It is something else, something beyond classical GR description. And those could potentially be engineered to transmit matter or just basic data through two different regions of spacetime.

Some error in the representation of the stationary black hole Penrose, it is strictly from an outside observer as depicted (22:00). Once a traveler crosses the horizon(22:25), passage of time is now horizontal. the light cone also rotates 90 degrees ccw. nothing changes as far as everything still leading to the singularity line, but the past light cone now can never accept light from the so called "white hole." The "infinite Future" from the perspective of being inside the horizon, is on the far left coroner. Unfortunately, the future light cone also asymptotes to infinite future as it approaches the left horizon line because the horizon line is the speed of light. Meaning even if you hold position just inside the horizon and traveled at light speed you can never pass back out of the horizon.  Outside the Horizon, your past light cone dose wonky things as it approaches the "white hole" boundary because you are stepping back in time but also getting closer to the horizon of the black hole.  Since light cannot come out of the horizon boundary, anything from this side of the past light cone appears black, hence black hole.
The notion of "not even light cannot escape" often leads to the misconception that one could escape if they travel faster than light. Curved spacetime is really weird. Once you cross that event horizon, the direction "outwards" simply does not exist anymore. Traveling faster than light in a regular spacetime may even be geometrically impossible: Take that spacetime diagram with one dimention of space and one of time, but *not* scaled such that light always travels at a 45¬∞ angle, this will be important later. If you're static in space, you only move through time. You could think of it as a vector with a value ("length") of _c._ that represents a velocity through spacetime. Stand still and you "move" at the speed of light through time only, but not space. Now, the faster you move in space, the slower you will move in time, i.e. that vector is now "tilting" into the space coordinate, but its value is still fixed at c. As a result, you will experience time at a slower rate. If you were a photon of light, you would move entirely through space at c and no longer experience time, which is what relativity theories also conclude.

I had an argument with my brother 20 years ago that lasts to this day about that last statement: "we have one universe, why can't we have two?"  It's pedantic, I know, but *by definition* THE universe is everything that exists, did exist, and will exist. It's a singleton. Of course, these are just words, and it's just a matter of explaining concepts from previous concepts, so I understand the utility, but the very idea of a "multiverse", which implies the existence of multiple universes, is fundamentally impossible, as there can only be one universe by the definition of the word "universe".  The problem is that we developed concepts without creating terms for the entities. Really, we should have created a term for the part/segment/region-time of the universe we occupy, and instead of crossing the edge (in space or time, either way) of the universe into another universe, we should be expanding what we think of as 'the universe' and the math is simply revealing other 'place-times' within the universe.  So no, we can't have two universes, but there's no reason the universe has to end at these 3+1 dimensions. In fact, it's a certainty that it doesn't!
to elaborate on the quite clever opening analogy, if the nemesis' rocket ship is to go into the black hole, my understanding was that the photons, reflected from the spaceship while it crosses the event horizon, essentially go into orbit around the black hole at light speed- it's not like they're frozen 'in place' as the video shows, of course, they're photons so they can only travel at c, but interestingly this forms essentially a 3-dimensional holographic map of what crossed the event horizon, in that it all reflects photons during this transit, and those photons start their orbit around the black hole. To perceive or detect those photons later would be to also destroy that holographic image and essentially replace it with the image of your photon detector, which itself will be also lost to the event horizon. So I'm curious how someone inside a tinted-glass rocket ship, someone (our 'nemesis' perhaps) thus insulated from photons reflecting to the outside, might ever get recorded in such orbital photons of the black hole? And would this holographic photon-record be able to remember long-term who crossed, or only short term as in the most recent crossings?
Hm, so gravity is not a force that is exerted, but curvature of space?  Thus planets circle arround stars not because they are attracted, but because space is bent arround the star in a circle and thus, moving in a straight, inert line through that space ends up beeing a circle.  But, if it was just that, then any given object would always be lead into a circle arround a star at the same rate. But they arent. Slower moving objects will get drawn in deeper, even to the point of crashing, and faster moving objects will be affected way less, only beeing diverted from their original path to a small degree and then go on.  If gravity was only bent space, it wouldn't matter how fast a passing-by object was, it would always follow the curvature of space arround the star in the same way. What we observe however is precisly the result of two forces, that excert their influence over time, and the larger force influencing the path of the object stronger: for slow moving objects, gravity pulls them in deeper, for faster moving objects, their directed potential wins and they get diverted only a little bit.  Curvature of space alone is not a working explanation for gravity.
Sabine Hossenfelder published a book called Lost In Math that criticises certain physicists for their obsession with solutions that are mathematically possible if one ignores certain key constraints. But we already know, for example, that even very basic equations are time-reversible yet the universe in which we actually exist is a one-way street. So playing math games and pretending that white holes, wormholes, etc. could be "real" ends up with Penrose's dreams about conformal spacetime "reimagining itself" trillions of years from now - a dream that carefully ignores a couple of important constraints existing in the real universe. A lot of sci-fi cowboys in space stuff has profited from this pseudo-science but ordinary people have been dramatically (sic) misled regarding what's actually possible. And this confusion between fantasy and reality leads to NASA squandering hundreds of billions of dollars that could have gone on real science but is directed toward pointless human missions to Mars, while the halfwit Musk will likewise squander billions on his own inane dream of a Mars colony that totally ignores the physical realities pertinent to such a silly venture.

I disagree with the statement that "from the perspective of an observer falling into a black hole, everything appears to be normal". From the perspective of a falling observer, time flow in the rest of the universe accelerates. This observer would see the rest of the universe getting older at an accelerated rate, until it reachs its end. And that where is the real flaw with this though experiment : it assumes that the black hole has an infinite lifespan. However that's not the case, black holes evaporate, and even if this process can take a huge amount of time, it doesn't matter when the flow of time accelerates infinitively from the perspective of the falling observer. This means that, as the observer is nearing the event horizon, the black hole evaporates and shrinks, so the observer can never actually reach this horizon until the black hole completely evaporates.  That is coherent with what a distant observer would see, but also get rid of the problem of the singularity at the center of the black hole : nothing can fall into it as everything falling onto the event horizon ends up frozen in time and unable to move, prisonner from the clutch of gravity until the black hole itself reachs the end of its life.

If there is really nothing going on at that surface as is suggested in this video and that it is just an artefact of a particular projection then it could not be the case that there is information encoded in this event horizon surface. Perhaps this is why the holographic principle is controversial?   Another point of concern on my part is that the original Schwarzschild solution is an extremely idealised mathematical fiction. We are basing our construction of a real physical space on this idealised mathematical point solution. Yet traditionally mathematical idealisations were adapted to fit with physical space, yet this black hole construction case, seems to reverse the usual order of ontological priority. Not to say it is or isn't correct, but it raises fundamental conceptual questions in philosophy of science as to the reality status of the phenomena concerned and how it can be confirmed, falsified or experienced.

General relativity and quantum mechanics will never be combined until we realize that each individual observer is observing them both at different moments in time. Because causality has a speed limit (c) every point in space where one observes it from will be the closest to the present moment. When one looks out into the universe they see the past which is made of particles (GR). When one tries to look at smaller and smaller sizes and distances, they are actually looking closer and closer to the present moment (QM). The wave property of particles appears when we start trying to predict the future of that particle. It is a probability wave because the future is probabilistic. Wave function collapse is what we perceive as the present moment and is what divides the past from the future.  GR is making measurements in the observed past and therefore, predictable.  It can predict the future but only from information collected from the past.  QM is attempting to make measurements of the unobserved future and therefore, unpredictable.  Only once a particle interacts with the present moment does it become predictable.  This is an observational interpretation of the mathematics we currently use based on the limited perspective we have with the experiments we choose to observe the universe with.

I think our real problem lies in the number of distinct tasks our brains can perform. We cannot conceptualise the entirety of geological time, let alone that of the universe at its smallest and largest increments mostly because our brains chunk information in ways that allow us to find food, make babies, and avoid death with minimal effort over roughly 85 years. It is incredible that we have manipulated our cognitive capacity for human survival to understand phenomena well beyond our immediate need, but there are physical limits to this; our neurons can only give way to so much elasticity. Sure, computers help render ideas such as infinity in humanly comprehensible concepts, but they cannot "witness" them for us. Transgressing the testable boundaries of our knowledge is fun for the imagination and has led us to landmark scientific discoveries, but not without dispensing more questions or even casting doubt over prior knowledge; this is the nature of our scientific method. It's why I like that we "put in our two cents" but only get "a penny for our thoughts"; for every investment of our intelligence, we will have an even larger debt to pay for any complete understanding of the universe. Doesn't mean we shouldn't keep asking the questions; we will just need to be satisfied with not knowing all the answers.

The statement at 9:00 is a common misconception that is taught by physics educators. Although it is true the the escape velocity at the schwarzschild radius is the speed of light, that has nothing to do with defining what a schwarzschild radius is. After all, an "escape velocity" by definition is the velocity that you'd have to start with in order to counteract the deceleration that you get from gravity in order to escape. This means that technically, even if the escape velocity somewhere was the speed of light, you could still escape it as long as you kept applying a force on the object so that the object doesn't stop moving before it manages to escape. This, for example, could be a rocket that has a thrust force counteracting the deceleration due to gravity.  What is different about the schwarzschild radius is that you CAN'T escape, even if you applied a constant force on the object. This property of it has nothing to do with its escape velocity, so it is very misleading to say that "the escape velocity at the schwarzschild radius is the speed of light" and use that to imply that that's why you can't escape the schwarzschild radius, which would be untrue. The youtube channel FloatHeadPhysics made a video quite recently about this exact topic called "Why can nothing escape a black hole?" which addresses this issue in physics education.

The greatest intellectual prison humanity has ever built was constructed not from steel or stone, but from a single word: time. From the moment our ancestors looked at the rising and setting sun and decided to call the interval between them a measurable thing, we locked ourselves into a conceptual framework that has quietly distorted nearly every branch of science, philosophy, and human understanding ever since. We did not discover time the way we discovered fire or gravity. We invented the concept, gave it a name, embedded it into our mathematics, elevated it into a dimension, and then spent millennia trying to understand, manipulate, and travel through something that may not exist in the way we imagine it at all. The ancient Egyptians used obelisks as sundials. The Greeks built water clocks. Newton declared time absolute and universal. Einstein declared it relative and bendable. And yet, across all these centuries and all these brilliant minds, not one framework has fully explained what time fundamentally is вАФ only how it behaves under certain conditions. Perhaps the reason we cannot find the bottom of time is because we are digging in the wrong ground entirely. Perhaps time is not a thing to be measured or traveled through, but a label we placed on something far more profound: the universal phenomenon of change propagating through a vibrational reality.

Interestingly, white holes could very well exist but unlike black holes, we would probably never be able to detect them. The problem with white holes is actually the same as for black holes, but obviously inverted. A person entering a black hole would appear to slow down infinitely as time is dilated from the perspective of an outside observer, while the person entering the black hole--should they be able to look back out--would see the outside universe accelerating toward infinite speed.  For a white hole, a person theoretically exiting would emerge to a universe already passing at infinite speed, and for everything outside the white hole, anything which emerged would do so completely frozen in time. Because the curve is reversed, eternity will elapse in the universe before an object reaches the center of a black hole, and eternity will elapse in the universe before an object can exit a white hole.  Despite this, it is not impossible for white holes to exist, they would simply never emit anything. We can identify black holes by the energy released as matter accelerates enough to escape before crossing the event horizon. A white hole, however, does not pull things toward it, it pushes, so anything passing by one would be slowed down, and then sent off into space with dramatically _reduced_ energy, making them far more challenging to locate, if not impossible.

The Unified Theory of Dark Energy and Quantum Black Holes This hypothesis proposes a radical alternative to the standard models of black holes and cosmology, asserting that black holes are the engines that generate Dark Energy and that the universe is static, not expanding. I. The Black Hole Mechanism: Dark Energy Generation This model defines a black hole as an object that replicates the extreme energy density of the Big Bang, altering the fundamental laws of particle physics at the event horizon. 1. Mass Acquisition: When an incoming photon (light) crosses the event horizon, it gains immense energyвАФan environment that temporarily replicates the conditions of the Big Bang. 2. Exotic Pair Production: This extreme energy causes the photon to break down into two massive particles: a positive particle with mass and a negative particle with mass (this specific pair production is a new, unproven quantum process). 3. Counterpart Loss (Capture): The positive particle is immediately drawn toward and sucked into the black hole, increasing its positive mass. 4. Quantum Escape (Mass Loss): Due to an unknown quantum law, the loss of its entangled positive counterpart forces the negative particle to become massless. 5. Emission: As a massless particle, it can now travel at the speed of light (c), which is the escape velocity at the event horizon. This negative, massless particle is expelled from the black hole.

This is simply false, and i don't know why physicists keep making this mistake, the laws of physics we have are not actually time reversal invariant, this is well known, consider the discovery of the violation of CP invariance, which was recognize in the decay of the netural K meson, the decay is not invariant if one changes a right-handed for a left-handed spatial orientation (parity) and changes positive for negative charge (charge conjugation). The CPT theorem tell us that any reasonable quantum theory will be invariant under parity-plus-charge-conjugation-plus-time-reversal, so the violation of CP implies a violation of T. This is a very clear case of science revealing a temporal orientation.  Besides the whole idea of "Time Reversal Invariant" has been misunderstood, because if you actually look into the details of it you will see that it does not show that there is no temporal direction,  because when you perform the time reversal operation you have to perform it on instantaneous states, this fact shows just the opposite of the lack of temporal directionality, since it demonstrates even for an instantaneous state, there is a fact about how it is oriented with respect to the direction of time.

Reversing time changes nothing about gravity, it is still attractive, planets can orbit around a white hole as they can around a black hole. Changing the sign of time in the equations changes the sign of the velocity but not of the acceleration.  If a stone falls into a black hole, as you said, from an external observer perspective, it would slow down so much that you'd never really see it crossing the horizon. If a stone now goes out of a white hole, the first part of its journey, near the horizon, would be ininitely slow. Finally, if a stone gets close to a white hole, it could not cross the horizon (by "definition"), and would rather just join the the other stones leaving the white hole very slowly (from an outside observer perspective again). As cool as this is its kinda just wrong. Wrong not in the sense that the math is wrong, but the interpretation of what the math means is wrong.  For instance, I stir my tea clockwise. If I then stir my tea counterclockwise would I say that time has reversed because I reversed all the trajectories of the tea molecules? Well no.  Even worse: when you realize how projection works you realize that you can make any bijective transformation that preserves the structureвА¶that in essence you can manipulate your coordinate system to get any interpretation for what is happening that you want.  So hereвАЩs whatвАЩs really happening: time is not trivially defined by flipping positive and negative signs. It is structured in particular the history of the past is embedded in the events that happen in the futureвА¶which is why you donвАЩt go back in time. Like in the tea example and reversing trajectoriesвА¶we know time didnвАЩt reverse stirring clockwise then counterclockwise, because embedded in this procedure we know that we stirred it clockwise THEN counterclockwise, time moves forwardвА¶only trajectories changed.   ItвАЩs all rather flawed in that these interpretations have a total disregard for the arbitrariness and subjectivity (the role of the observer and interpreter) of mathematics. That doesnвАЩt make math less useful, but in fact more beautiful and arguably way more useful and universal. It all sounds quite magical, like 'falling into space' and magical things happen ... like in 'The Black Hole' by Disney.  But the reality is that you simply fall toward the center of the black hole....in the exact same way you fall toward the center of the Earth. The difference is that as you accelerate towards the center of the black hole.  My intuition tells me that you fall toward the center, just like falling to the center of the Earth -- as though you jumped out of an airplane.  If you are the only object currently falling to the center, you would arrive at the center and land on top of any objects not yet collapsed into the singularity.  You would die at that point. If there are other objects currently falling, you would essentially be merged with them, like being inside a huge combustion chamber of an engine with a massive piston squeezing you all together until your are all one lump. 32:18  It has always been known that the space-time metrics created by rotating and static bodies differ significantly from each other in GR.* In the first (stationary) case, g(ik) depend on the angular velocity of rotation of the body w=d—Д/dt (—Д is the azimuth angle), the sign of which changes when reflected x(0)~-x(0), but when simultaneously replaced with —Д~-—Д, the angular velocity does not change. By the way, Kerr's solution speculates on this, however, the nature of the gravitational field depends on the sign of the angular velocity and no coordinate transformation can turn all g(ik) to zero and save the invariance of the interval: g(01)=g(02), but g(03)=g(0—Д)... not =0.¬† And that's it.¬† P.S.The Kerr metric is only a particular solution corresponding to a certain distribution of masses in a rotating body. Moreover, this metric contains a trick: when we change only the sign of time or only the sign of the azimuthal angle, the invariance of the metric is violated. This is as it should be, since such transformations are equivalent to changing the direction of rotation, and the nature of the gravitational field depends on the sign of the angular velocity. Obviously, if a<r(G)/2, the metric has a singularity when a~0 passes into the Schwarzschild singularity at the point r=0 and on the sphere r=r(G). When a>r(G), then there is a singularity on the ring with r=0. вАФ---------- *) - But not in Newtonian gravity: there's no problem with that here; and with a hint that squint and chiral are different things.

True, relativity doesn't care about the future or the past, or more specifically which one is which, because it simply doesn't care. Relativity is a mathematical construct. For that matter space and time respectively... That is to say, the dimensions we perceive, are all constructs. In reality, they are all the same thing. People like to refer to them as fields, although I don't think that that does them proper justice.   As for white holes, the white hole is what you would observe just on the other side of the event horizon of the black hole. Initially, it would be infinitely small. The way that you would observe it however would be to provide force, progressing towards infinity, at an angle directly tangent to the singularity. The more force you applied, the larger the white hole would appear in front of you. The mass that it would be ejecting is the material that is pulled from the event horizon. The force that it would push on you is effectively the force that you would be feeling from behind you. Remember, we're dealing with space-time in four dimensions here. You think of the black hole as a sphere, and you perceive yourself on being on the inside of a sphere. That is an improper perception. On the inside of the event horizon of the black hole, you would quickly discover that you could effectively orbit the entire universe. Of course that would not last for very long. You would never even have the opportunity to make one orbit, because you would never possess the energy to be able to do it. That white hole would keep pushing you farther and farther away.
Einstein got close. Objects with a large enough mass simply generate a larger field, if an object exists within the larger mass object's gravity field those objects are attracted to the larger object and interact through the field. The moon is attracted to the Earth, the Earth has no attraction to the Moon.  At some point physicists lost their way and have been compounding absurd ideas on top of absurd foundations, so not much progress has been made on the fundamental classical scale of physics since we went down the wrong road, as there's not much incentive to perfect something that works close enough where we need it. I'm not sure what the original errors were exactly because people have added their own modern day absurdities on top of what those thinkers originally thought, but the primary cause of confusion and error is the misguided belief that time physically exists - it doesn't, it's just a really useful way to discretely talk about motion, in the same way that numbers are a really useful way to discretely talk about quantities, even though numbers don't physically exist either. So, since time and numbers don't physically exist, you definitely can't slow down or speed up time - only slow down or speed up motion of something.   Cheat code: Time does not exist, motion and differential density exist. So "spacetime" definitely doesn't exist. The reality is that identical volumes in space can have different densities - this concept of mass being able to occupy the same volume is not in the common person's imagination yet but one day it will be common knowledge.
